'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.

His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.

It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.

=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.

=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Kokuzō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kumano Kokuzō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>

During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.

During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

revision.text:
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

revision.text:
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>

revision.text:
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}}== See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}} == See Also! == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}} == See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References ==

=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}} == See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
== References ==
{{reflist}} == See Also == {{Col| * [[Kumano Province]] * [[Muro District]] * [[Kinan]] | * [[Nigihayahi]] * [[Kusunoki clan]] * [[Southern Court (Japan)]] }}

'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|ja|醍醐天皇}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} to the east and {{ill|Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|ja|熊野本宮大社}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|ja|熊野速玉大社}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|ja|下里古墳}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇}}, achieving the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Nanbokuchō period|ja|南北朝時代}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
