<!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--><!--Kumano Kokuzō--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11568883}} [[Category:Clans of Kii Province]] [[Category:History of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kii Province]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Kusunoki clan]] [[Category:Mononobe clan]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野国造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:熊野国造]]
'''Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (熊野国造, Governor of Kumano) was the title of the ruler who governed the Kumano Province, which later became part of Kii Province in Japan.
== Overview == === Ancestry === * According to the ''{{ill|Kujiki|en|Kujiki|qid=Q2716751|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|1=Kujiki}}'' in the "Kokuzō Hongi" section, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|en|Emperor Seimu|qid=Q316672|lt=Emperor Seimu|1=Emperor Seimu}}, {{ill|Ōato Ashinai|ja|大阿刀足尼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōato Ashinai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a fifth-generation descendant of {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニギハヤヒ}}, was appointed as the Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko. * The ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'' under "Yamashiro (Divine Origin, Heavenly Deities)" lists the {{ill|Kumano Muraji|ja|熊野連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Muraji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting their descent from Nigihayahi’s grandson Aji-Nigita-no-Mikoto.<ref>The original surname was likely "Kumano Ason," as indicated by historical notes about the lineage receiving the "Muraji" title later.</ref>
=== Clan === The {{ill|Kumano clan|ja|熊野氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (熊野氏, surname: ''Na'') was a prominent clan. In the Nara period, notable figures such as {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano no Hirohama|1=Kumano no Hirohama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109287645}} emerged from this lineage.
The "Wada Clan Genealogy" indicates that after the incorporation of Kumano Province into Kii Province, clan members served as ''daimyō'' of Muro District and hereditary priests of {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|en|Emperor Daigo|qid=Q329753|lt=Emperor Daigo|1=Emperor Daigo}}, Kumano no Hirokata, also known as {{ill|Tachibana no Hirokata|ja|橘広方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana no Hirokata|1=Tachibana no Hirokata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q20041240}}, adopted the Tachibana surname. His descendants later formed the Wada clan and played significant roles in local governance and religious affairs.
=== Governance === The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko governed Kumano Province, which corresponded to the southern region of modern-day {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It was bordered by {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} to the east and {{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|lt=Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 紀伊国造}} to the northwest.
== Shrine == The clan's tutelary shrine was likely {{ill|Kumano Hongū Taisha|en|Kumano Hongū Taisha|qid=Q705035|lt=Kumano Hongū Taisha|1=Kumano Hongū Taisha}}, a prominent Shinto shrine and one of the {{ill|Ninja Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninja Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Myōjin Taisha|qid=Q9610964}} in the region.
=== Related Shrines === * {{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Kumano Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}} (Shingū, Wakayama Prefecture)
== Burial Site == The clan’s burial site may include the {{ill|Shimosato Kofun|en|Shimosato Kofun|qid=Q11361369|lt=Shimosato Kofun|1=Shimosato Kofun}}, a keyhole-shaped tumulus dating to the late 4th or early 5th century, the only such tomb in the southern Kii Peninsula.
== Descendants == * {{ill|Kumano no Hirohama|ja|熊野広浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano no Hirohama|1=Kumano no Hirohama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109287645}} served as a court official during the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} through {{ill|Empress Shōtoku|ja|称徳天皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Shōtoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 孝謙天皇|1=Empress Kōken|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q232955}}, achieving the Rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, before his death in 769 CE. * During the Heian period, the clan produced notable figures such as the head of the Masago clan, who was known as the "Elder of Masago." * The Kumano no Kuni no Miyatsuko lineage is also linked to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanbokuchō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} and figures such as the {{ill|Wada clan (Kawachi)|ja|河内和田氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada clan (Kawachi)|1=Q65272223|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65272223}}, said to share descent with the {{ill|Kusunoki clan|ja|楠木氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki clan|1=Kusunoki clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11541208}}.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ōta Ryō|title=Sei Shi Kakei Daijiten|year=1935|volume=2|pages=2144–2145|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130938/165}}</ref>
